original_hypothesis.title: Block Time Drift Causes epochTime to Exceed block.timestamp, Zeroing All Gauge Weights
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: epochTime in update_market is estimated using averageBlockTime and referenceBlockNumber. If actual block production is faster than estimated, epochTime drifts ahead of block.timestamp. GaugeController._get_total only fills points_total up to block.timestamp. When epochTime exceeds block.timestamp by >1 week, gauge_relative_weight_write looks up an unfilled future week where points_total=0, returning weight=0. All market rewards silently become zero.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The Canto chain block time may fluctuate. A 3-4% drift over months causes epochTime to lead block.timestamp by a full week, at which point all gauge weights read as 0. This silently destroys all CANTO reward distributions until governance manually corrects the block time parameters.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Deploy.sol sets no block time parameters; defaults in LendingLedger constructor: averageBlockTime=5700, referenceBlockNumber=block.number, referenceBlockTime=block.timestamp

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000 — no validation that epochTime <= block.timestamp; GaugeController returns 0 weight for future timestamps

preconditions: Actual average block time is faster than the configured averageBlockTime
Sufficient blocks have passed for drift to exceed one week (~3+ months at 3% drift)
Governance has not called setBlockTimeParameters to correct the drift

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If actual=5.5s, configured=5.7s → drift=0.2s/block → after 3M blocks (~6 months), drift = 600,000s ≈ 1 week → gauge weights return 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural block time variation silently zeros all reward distributions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; all users lose rewards for affected epochs; recovery requires governance intervention

economic_questions: What is the actual Canto chain block time variance?
How quickly would drift accumulate to exceed one week?
Are there monitoring mechanisms to detect the drift?
validation_requirements: Compare estimated epochTime vs actual block.timestamp at various block counts
Test gauge_relative_weight_write return value when time > block.timestamp
Verify that once drift occurs, all markets simultaneously lose rewards

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - natural occurrence, not direct attack
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - loss event, not profit extraction

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After months of uncorrected block time drift
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total reward distribution failure across all markets

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All liquidity providers across all markets
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All CANTO emissions during the drift period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Canto chain actual block times, averageBlockTime=5700
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate epochTime drift over realistic timeframes

validation_result.mechanism: epochTime future drift causing zero gauge weights
validation_result.impact_summary: When block production is faster than configured averageBlockTime, epochTime drifts ahead of block.timestamp. Once drift exceeds ~1 week, all gauge weight lookups return 0, silently zeroing all CANTO reward distributions across all markets.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability is a missing validation in LendingLedger.update_market. The epochTime computation uses the configured averageBlockTime (default 5700ms) and current block.number to estimate the current timestamp. If actual block production is faster than configured (e.g., 5.5s actual vs 5.7s configured), epochTime will progressively exceed block.timestamp. GaugeController._get_total() only fills points_total up to approximately block.timestamp + WEEK. When epochTime exceeds block.timestamp by more than one week, _gauge_relative_weight looks up points_total at a future week that was never filled, finds 0, and returns 0 weight. This causes the accCantoPerShare accumulation to be zero for all markets, silently destroying all reward distributions. The defect is the absence of a cap or validation on epochTime relative to block.timestamp.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code path is fully traceable from update_market through gauge_relative_weight_write to _gauge_relative_weight. The epochTime formula at LendingLedger.sol:83-87 has no bounds check. GaugeController._get_total() at lines 130-152 only fills points_total up to one week past block.timestamp. _gauge_relative_weight at lines 178-188 returns 0 when points_total[t] == 0. The defect is present in the source code with no mitigating code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: epochTime is computed from block.number and averageBlockTime with no validation that the result is <= block.timestamp. If averageBlockTime overestimates the actual block time, epochTime will exceed block.timestamp, causing gauge weight lookups to query unfilled future timestamps.
When _time is a future timestamp beyond where _get_total has filled, points_total[t] is 0, causing the function to return 0 weight regardless of actual gauge votes.
_get_total only fills points_total up to the first week boundary past block.timestamp. Any lookup at a week boundary further in the future will find points_total = 0.

calling_sequence: 1. update_market is called (either directly or via sync_ledger/claim)
2. epochTime is computed at line 83-87 using block.number and averageBlockTime
4. gauge_relative_weight_write calls _get_weight and _get_total, filling data up to block.timestamp + WEEK
6. _gauge_relative_weight floors epochTime to WEEK boundary, looks up points_total[t]
7. If epochTime > block.timestamp + WEEK, points_total[t] == 0, function returns 0
8. Back in update_market, accCantoPerShare += (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock[epoch] * 0) / marketSupply == 0
9. No rewards accumulate for any market in this epoch
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: update_market computes epochTime at LendingLedger.sol:83-87 with no cap relative to block.timestamp. This epochTime is passed to gauge_relative_weight_write (line 90). Inside GaugeController, _get_total() (lines 130-152) fills points_total only up to block.timestamp + WEEK via the loop break condition at line 140. Then _gauge_relative_weight (lines 178-188) looks up points_total at the week-floored epochTime. When epochTime exceeds block.timestamp by more than WEEK, the lookup finds 0, and the function returns 0.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol calls `new LendingLedger(address(gaugeController), governance)` with no additional configuration of block time parameters. The constructor sets referenceBlockNumber = block.number and referenceBlockTime = block.timestamp. averageBlockTime defaults to 5700 as a class-level declaration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an active attack but a natural degradation scenario. If the Canto chain produces blocks faster than 5.7s on average (e.g., 5.5s), after approximately 3M blocks (~6 months), the epochTime will lead block.timestamp by ~1 week. At that point: (1) epochTime floored to WEEK gives a future week boundary, (2) _get_total only fills up to block.timestamp + WEEK, (3) points_total at the future week is 0, (4) gauge_relative_weight returns 0, (5) no rewards accrue for any market. The scenario is realistic for any blockchain where block times naturally fluctuate. The 3.5% drift assumed (5.7 vs 5.5) is within normal variance ranges for blockchain networks.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. When epochTime exceeds block.timestamp by >1 week, ALL markets simultaneously receive zero gauge weights, meaning zero CANTO rewards accumulate. This affects every liquidity provider across every market. Lost rewards during the drift period cannot be retroactively recovered because accCantoPerShare is permanently recorded as having not increased during those blocks. The issue is silent (no reverts, no events indicating failure), making detection difficult without active monitoring. Recovery requires governance intervention via setBlockTimeParameters, but rewards for the drift period are permanently lost.
recommended_mitigations: Add a cap in update_market: `epochTime = Math.min(epochTime, block.timestamp);` to prevent future timestamp lookups
Use block.timestamp directly instead of estimating it from block.number, eliminating the drift issue entirely
Add a require or assertion that epochTime <= block.timestamp + WEEK as a safety check
Add an event emission when epochTime diverges significantly from block.timestamp to enable monitoring
Consider computing epochTime per-iteration using the loop variable `i` instead of `block.number` to also fix the stale-weight-for-historical-epochs issue (INV-TIME-001)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts in a Foundry test. 2. Set averageBlockTime to 5700 (default). 3. Create a veCANTO lock, add a gauge, vote for it. 4. Whitelist a market, deposit into it. 5. Use vm.roll to advance block.number by 3M blocks (simulating ~6 months of fast blocks). 6. Use vm.warp to advance block.timestamp by 3M * 5.5 seconds (actual time). 7. Call update_market and verify that accCantoPerShare did not increase. 8. Verify gauge_relative_weight_write returns 0 for the computed epochTime. 9. Confirm that epochTime > block.timestamp + WEEK.
uncertainties: The actual Canto chain block time variance is not verified in scope - the severity depends on real-world block production rates
Governance may proactively monitor and correct drift via setBlockTimeParameters before the threshold is reached
The exact time to reach the critical 1-week drift depends on the magnitude and consistency of the block time discrepancy
additional_testing_needed: Measure actual Canto mainnet block time statistics to determine realistic drift rate
Test with various drift magnitudes to determine the exact threshold where rewards become zero
Verify that the fix (capping epochTime at block.timestamp) doesn't introduce other issues with historical epoch processing
Test whether partial drift (< 1 week) causes incorrect but non-zero weights, potentially resulting in wrong reward distribution even before total failure
